slides.title: Update Variables Without Rewriting Them
slides.body: Every program tracks **changing values** — scores, totals, counters. Assignment operators are Python's tools for updating a variable without starting from scratch.
slides.tip: You already know = — now meet its whole family of shortcuts.

slides.title: = Puts a Value in a Box
slides.body: Think of a variable as a labeled box. `=` is the action of placing something inside it — it means **store this value**, not "is equal to".
slides.tip: To *check* equality you use == (two equals signs) — completely different!

slides.title: The Long Way vs. the Shortcut
slides.body: `score = score + 5` works, but `score += 5` says the same thing in half the space. These **augmented assignment operators** combine an operation and an assignment into one step.
slides.tip: Think of += like a tally counter button — press it and the number goes up automatically.

slides.title: Six More Shortcuts in the Family
slides.body: Python has seven augmented operators in total: `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, `//=`, `%=`, and `**=`. Each one updates the variable **in place**.
slides.tip: /= always gives a float result; use //= if you want a whole number.

slides.title: You've Got the Update Power!
slides.body: You can now update any variable — numbers, strings, even lists — cleanly and efficiently. Always **initialize** a variable before using `+=` on it, and you're good to go.
